description: The study of equations relating functions to their derivatives. Encompasses ordinary differential equations (ODEs), systems of ODEs, Laplace transform methods, series solutions, and partial differential equations (PDEs).

concepts.name: Ordinary Differential Equation
concepts.description: An equation involving an unknown function of one variable and its derivatives. The order is the highest derivative present; the degree is the power of that highest derivative.

concepts.name: Order of a Differential Equation
concepts.description: The order of an ODE is determined by the highest derivative appearing in it. A first-order ODE involves only y′; second-order involves y″, etc.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.1

concepts.name: Linearity of Differential Equations
concepts.description: An ODE is linear if it can be written as a linear combination of y and its derivatives with coefficients depending only on the independent variable. Nonlinear ODEs include products of y and its derivatives.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.1

concepts.name: Types of Solutions
concepts.description: A general solution contains arbitrary constants equal in number to the ODE order. A particular solution fixes those constants from initial/boundary conditions. Singular solutions are not obtainable from the general solution.
references: Tenenbaum & Pollard, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.2

concepts.name: Initial Value Problem
concepts.description: An ODE together with conditions specifying the value of the solution and its derivatives at a single point (the initial point). Determines a unique solution under appropriate smoothness conditions.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.2

concepts.name: Existence and Uniqueness Theorem
concepts.description: If f(x,y) and ∂f/∂y are continuous in a rectangle around (x₀,y₀), then the IVP y′=f(x,y), y(x₀)=y₀ has a unique solution in some interval around x₀. Proved via Picard iteration.
references: Coddington & Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.1

concepts.name: First-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE involving only the first derivative y′. Solution methods include separation of variables, integrating factors, exact equations, substitution, and Bernoulli's equation technique.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.2

concepts.name: Separable Differential Equation
concepts.description: An ODE of the form dy/dx = g(x)h(y); rearranged as dy/h(y) = g(x)dx and integrated on both sides. One of the most elementary solution techniques.

concepts.name: Linear First-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE of the form y′ + P(x)y = Q(x). Solved by multiplying by the integrating factor μ(x)=e^{∫P dx}, making the left side an exact derivative.

concepts.name: Exact Differential Equation
concepts.description: An ODE M(x,y)dx + N(x,y)dy = 0 where ∂M/∂y = ∂N/∂x. Solution is found by identifying a potential function F such that dF=0.
references: Tenenbaum & Pollard, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.4

concepts.name: Bernoulli Equation
concepts.description: An ODE of the form y′ + P(x)y = Q(x)yⁿ. Linearized by the substitution v = y^{1-n}, converting to a linear first-order ODE in v.

concepts.name: Homogeneous First-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE of the form dy/dx = F(y/x). Solved by the substitution v = y/x, transforming it into a separable equation in v and x.
references: Tenenbaum & Pollard, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.3

concepts.name: Slope Field
concepts.description: A graphical representation of a first-order ODE by drawing short tangent-line segments at a grid of points (x,y), revealing the qualitative behavior of solutions without solving explicitly.

concepts.name: Euler's Method
concepts.description: The simplest numerical method for ODEs: step forward in x by h, updating y by y_{n+1} = y_n + h·f(x_n, y_n). Error per step O(h²); global error O(h).

concepts.name: Second-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE involving the second derivative y″. Of primary physical importance (Newton's second law, harmonic oscillators, beam equations). Standard form: y″ + P(x)y′ + Q(x)y = G(x).
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.3

concepts.name: Second-Order Linear ODE
concepts.description: An ODE y″ + P(x)y′ + Q(x)y = G(x). When G=0 it is homogeneous; when G≠0 it is nonhomogeneous. Solutions form a vector space (superposition principle applies for homogeneous case).

concepts.name: Homogeneous Second-Order Linear ODE
concepts.description: The equation y″ + P(x)y′ + Q(x)y = 0. Two linearly independent solutions y₁, y₂ form a fundamental set; general solution is c₁y₁ + c₂y₂. Linearity determined by the Wronskian.

concepts.name: Wronskian
concepts.description: W(y₁,y₂) = y₁y₂′ − y₁′y₂; nonzero at some point iff y₁ and y₂ are linearly independent. Abel's theorem gives W(x) = W(x₀)exp(−∫P dx).

concepts.name: Characteristic Equation
concepts.description: For constant-coefficient ODE ay″+by′+cy=0, substituting y=e^{rx} yields the characteristic equation ar²+br+c=0. The three cases (distinct real, repeated, complex roots) yield distinct solution forms.

concepts.name: Method of Undetermined Coefficients
concepts.description: A technique for finding a particular solution to ay″+by′+cy=G(x) when G(x) is a polynomial, exponential, sine, cosine, or product thereof. Assumes a trial solution of the same form as G(x) and solves for unknown coefficients.

concepts.name: Variation of Parameters
concepts.description: A general technique for finding particular solutions to y″+P(x)y′+Q(x)y=G(x) using the known fundamental set {y₁,y₂}. Sets y_p = u₁y₁+u₂y₂ and imposes conditions to determine u₁′,u₂′ via the Wronskian.

concepts.name: Harmonic Oscillator
concepts.description: The ODE my″+by′+ky=F(t) models spring-mass-damper systems. Cases: undamped (b=0), underdamped (b²<4mk), critically damped (b²=4mk), overdamped (b²>4mk). Each case determines the transient behavior.

concepts.name: Resonance
concepts.description: When the forcing frequency matches the natural frequency of an undamped oscillator, the amplitude grows without bound (pure resonance). In damped systems, resonance occurs at a slightly different frequency (practical resonance).

concepts.name: Higher-Order Linear ODE
concepts.description: An nth-order linear ODE has n linearly independent solutions forming an n-dimensional solution space. Constant-coefficient case solved by characteristic polynomial of degree n; methods generalize from second-order case.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.4

concepts.name: Laplace Transform
concepts.description: The integral transform ℒ{f}(s) = ∫₀^∞ e^{−st}f(t)dt converting differential equations into algebraic equations in s. Transforms derivatives into polynomial multiplication in s.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.6

concepts.name: Laplace Transform Properties
concepts.description: Key properties: linearity (ℒ{af+bg}=aℒ{f}+bℒ{g}), first shifting theorem (ℒ{e^{at}f}(s)=F(s−a)), second shifting theorem (Heaviside step), derivative rule (ℒ{f′}=sF(s)−f(0)), convolution.

concepts.name: Inverse Laplace Transform
concepts.description: The process of recovering f(t) from its Laplace transform F(s). Standard technique: use partial fraction decomposition to match to known transform pairs from tables.

concepts.name: Solving ODEs with Laplace Transform
concepts.description: Workflow: take Laplace transform of ODE to get algebraic equation in Y(s), solve for Y(s), take inverse Laplace to obtain y(t). Initial conditions are automatically incorporated in the transform step.

concepts.name: Convolution Theorem (Laplace)
concepts.description: ℒ{f*g}(s) = F(s)·G(s), where (f*g)(t)=∫₀^t f(τ)g(t−τ)dτ is the convolution. Allows recovery of the inverse Laplace of a product without full partial-fraction decomposition.

concepts.name: Systems of ODEs
concepts.description: A set of ODEs involving multiple dependent variables: x′=Ax+b (linear) or x′=f(x) (nonlinear). First-order systems can represent nth-order scalar ODEs via state-vector reduction.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.7

concepts.name: Matrix Method for Linear Systems
concepts.description: For x′=Ax, solutions are of the form x=e^{λt}v where (λ,v) are eigenvalue-eigenvector pairs of A. Three cases: distinct real, repeated, complex conjugate eigenvalues — each producing a distinct fundamental matrix.

concepts.name: Phase Plane Analysis
concepts.description: For 2D autonomous systems x′=f(x,y), y′=g(x,y), trajectories in the xy-plane (phase plane) reveal global solution behavior. Equilibria classified by eigenvalue structure: nodes, spirals, saddles, centers.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.5–6

concepts.name: Stability Analysis
concepts.description: Classification of equilibria of ODEs: stable (Lyapunov), asymptotically stable, or unstable. Linearization near equilibria using the Jacobian determines local stability; Lyapunov functions give global results.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.6; Perko, Differential Equations and Dynamical Systems

concepts.name: Series Solution of ODEs
concepts.description: Assumes y=∑aₙxⁿ and substitutes into the ODE to determine the recurrence relation for coefficients aₙ. Valid when x=0 is an ordinary point of the ODE.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.5

concepts.name: Frobenius Method
concepts.description: For ODEs with a regular singular point at x=0, seeks solutions of the form y=xʳ∑aₙxⁿ. The indicial equation determines r. Three subcases based on root difference: distinct non-integer, integer, or equal roots.

concepts.name: Bessel's Equation
concepts.description: x²y″+xy′+(x²−ν²)y=0; solutions are Bessel functions Jᵥ(x) and Yᵥ(x). Arise in problems with cylindrical symmetry; Jᵥ regular at origin, Yᵥ singular.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, §5.7; Watson, Bessel Functions

concepts.name: Legendre's Equation
concepts.description: (1−x²)y″−2xy′+n(n+1)y=0; polynomial solutions are Legendre polynomials Pₙ(x). Arise in problems with spherical symmetry. Form an orthogonal basis on [−1,1].

concepts.name: Sturm-Liouville Theory
concepts.description: The boundary value problem (p(x)y′)′+q(x)y+λw(x)y=0 with boundary conditions. Has countably many eigenvalues λₙ and orthogonal eigenfunctions φₙ forming a complete set in L²([a,b],w).
references: Birkhoff & Rota, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.10

concepts.name: Eigenfunction Expansion
concepts.description: Expanding a function f in terms of eigenfunctions φₙ of a Sturm-Liouville problem: f(x)=∑cₙφₙ(x) with cₙ=⟨f,φₙ⟩/⟨φₙ,φₙ⟩. Generalizes Fourier series to arbitrary weighted inner products.
references: Birkhoff & Rota, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.10

concepts.name: Boundary Value Problem
concepts.description: An ODE with conditions specified at two or more distinct points. Unlike IVPs, existence and uniqueness are not guaranteed; a BVP may have no solution, a unique solution, or infinitely many solutions.

concepts.name: Fourier Series
concepts.description: Represents a periodic function as f(x)=a₀/2+∑(aₙcos(nπx/L)+bₙsin(nπx/L)), with coefficients computed by orthogonality integrals. Cornerstone of classical and modern analysis.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.10; Körner, Fourier Analysis

concepts.name: Convergence of Fourier Series
concepts.description: Dirichlet's theorem: if f is piecewise smooth, the Fourier series converges to f(x) at continuity points and to [f(x⁺)+f(x⁻)]/2 at jumps. Near jump discontinuities, overshoot is ~9% (Gibbs phenomenon).

concepts.name: Fourier Sine and Cosine Series
concepts.description: On [0,L], a function can be extended as an odd function (yielding a pure sine series) or an even function (yielding a pure cosine series), matching different boundary conditions in PDE applications.

concepts.name: Fourier Transform
concepts.description: The integral transform f̂(ω)=∫_{-∞}^∞ f(t)e^{−iωt}dt for non-periodic functions. Converts differentiation to multiplication by iω; used for solving ODEs/PDEs on the whole real line.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.10; Folland, Fourier Analysis

concepts.name: Partial Differential Equation
concepts.description: An equation involving partial derivatives of an unknown function of several variables. Classified (for linear second-order) as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic based on the discriminant of the principal part.
references: Evans, Partial Differential Equations; Strauss, Partial Differential Equations

concepts.name: Classification of Second-Order PDEs
concepts.description: For Au_{xx}+Bu_{xy}+Cu_{yy}+lower=0, the discriminant B²−4AC determines: elliptic (<0), parabolic (=0), hyperbolic (>0). Each class has distinct mathematical structure and physical interpretation.

concepts.name: Separation of Variables (PDE)
concepts.description: Assumes u(x,t)=X(x)T(t) (or analogous in other variables), splitting the PDE into ODEs for each factor, linked by a separation constant. Yields eigenvalue problems for X, then Fourier series expansions.

concepts.name: Heat Equation
concepts.description: u_t = k·u_{xx} (parabolic). Models heat diffusion and Brownian motion. Solved on finite intervals by separation of variables + Fourier series; on the full line by Fourier transform giving Gaussian kernel solution.
references: Strauss, Partial Differential Equations, Ch.1; Evans, PDE, Ch.2

concepts.name: Wave Equation
concepts.description: u_{tt} = c²u_{xx} (hyperbolic). Models string vibrations, acoustics, and light. D'Alembert's solution u=f(x+ct)+g(x−ct) on the infinite line; Fourier series on finite intervals.

concepts.name: Laplace's Equation
concepts.description: ∇²u = u_{xx}+u_{yy} = 0 (elliptic). Solutions are harmonic functions, satisfying the mean value property and maximum principle. Solved on disks/rectangles by separation; on complex domains by conformal mapping.
references: Evans, PDE, Ch.2; Strauss, PDE, Ch.6

concepts.name: Harmonic Functions
concepts.description: Functions satisfying ∇²u=0. Key properties: mean value property (value at a point equals average over any surrounding sphere/circle), maximum/minimum principle (extrema only on boundary), uniqueness of Dirichlet problem.

concepts.name: Poisson's Equation
concepts.description: ∇²u = f; the nonhomogeneous version of Laplace's equation. Arises in electrostatics (Poisson's equation for potential), Newtonian gravity. Solved by Green's functions when f is a source term.

concepts.name: Green's Function
concepts.description: The Green's function G(x,ξ) is the response to a unit point source: LG=δ(x−ξ). Allows expressing solutions to Lu=f as u(x)=∫G(x,ξ)f(ξ)dξ. Encodes both operator and boundary conditions.
references: Evans, PDE, §2.4; Stakgold & Holst, Green's Functions and Boundary Value Problems

concepts.name: Nonlinear ODE
concepts.description: An ODE in which the unknown function or its derivatives appear nonlinearly. General analytic solutions rarely exist; qualitative theory (phase plane, Lyapunov, bifurcation) and numerical methods are primary tools.

concepts.name: Bifurcation Theory
concepts.description: A bifurcation is a qualitative change in the solution structure of a system as a parameter crosses a critical value. Classic types: saddle-node, pitchfork, transcritical, Hopf. Each has a canonical normal form.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.3; Guckenheimer & Holmes, Nonlinear Oscillations

concepts.name: Chaotic Dynamics
concepts.description: Deterministic systems exhibiting sensitive dependence on initial conditions: nearby trajectories diverge exponentially (positive Lyapunov exponent). Lorenz attractor is the canonical example. Not the same as randomness.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.9–12
